entries.title: *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Aldus, 1499) — woodcut (leaf 4)
entries.creator: Francesco Colonna (attrib.); printed by Aldus Manutius
entries.rights: Public domain. 1499 Aldine edition via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls from the opening of Poliphilo's dream, where the lover loses himself in a dark wood and emerges among the first marvels of the visionary landscape.

## Iconography
At this point near the beginning of the dream, the woodcuts of the *Hypnerotomachia* render the visionary's first wanderings: the dark wood and the thirst that drives him on, the wellspring and the wild country, and the dawning architecture of an enchanted land. Hatching is sparing and the line is famously pure, leaving large fields of white incunabular page. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
The opening sequence stages the descent into dream as a Neoplatonic *itinerarium* in which the soul, lost in the [[motif:dark wood|silva]] of the senses, sets out toward beauty. The 1499 cuts, anonymous but long admired, fuse Albertian architectural clarity with the erotic-initiatory frame that Francesco Colonna's text demands.

## For artists & game designers
The 1499 *Hypnerotomachia* woodcut style, pure outline, minimal hatching, antique architecture and ceremonious figures on open white ground, is an ideal reference for a dream-logic, hermetic-Renaissance visual register: temples, triumphs, hieroglyphic friezes and processions rendered as clean, almost diagrammatic line.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin (trans.), *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili: The Strife of Love in a Dream* (Thames & Hudson, 1999).

entries.title: *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Aldus, 1499) — woodcut (leaf 31)
entries.creator: Francesco Colonna (attrib.); printed by Aldus Manutius
entries.rights: Public domain. 1499 Aldine edition via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls among the great architectural marvels of Poliphilo's journey: the colossal portal, the pyramid-and-obelisk, the ruins and hieroglyphic friezes.

## Iconography
In this stretch the *Hypnerotomachia*'s celebrated architectural woodcuts dominate: a colossal gateway and triumphal portal, the stepped pyramid surmounted by an obelisk and bronze figure, broken classical ruins, inscribed tablets, and friezes of Egyptian-style hieroglyphs. The draughtsman renders entablature, moulding and perspective with the measured precision of a Renaissance architectural treatise. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
These are among the most influential images in the book: their hieroglyphic frieze and antique architecture shaped Renaissance ideas of the [[motif:hieroglyph|hieroglyph]] as a silent, sacred picture-writing, feeding into Pierio Valeriano and later emblem theory. Architecture here is itself allegory, proportion, ruin and inscription read as a hermetic alphabet the dreamer must decipher.

entries.title: *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Aldus, 1499) — woodcut (leaf 92)
entries.creator: Francesco Colonna (attrib.); printed by Aldus Manutius
entries.rights: Public domain. 1499 Aldine edition via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls within the episodes of the nymphs, where the lover is met by the five nymphs of the senses and led through baths, fountains and gardens.

## Iconography
Here the woodcuts depict the nymphs who guide and tempt Poliphilo: graceful female figures in classical drapery beside fountains, baths and pergolas, leading the dreamer through pleasances toward the queenly presences of the dream. Drapery falls in clean parallel folds; the figures are slender and ceremonious. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
The five nymphs are traditionally read as the five senses, conducting the soul from sensory delight toward intellectual and divine beauty in a Neoplatonic ascent. Their gardens and fountains supply the erotic-contemplative imagery that made the *Hypnerotomachia* a quarry for later allegorists of love and the senses.

## For artists & game designers

This leaf falls within the episodes of the nymphs, where the lover is met by the five nymphs of the senses and led through baths, fountains and gardens.

## Iconography
Here the woodcuts depict the nymphs who guide and tempt Poliphilo: graceful female figures in classical drapery beside fountains, baths and pergolas, leading the dreamer through pleasances toward the queenly presences of the dream. Drapery falls in clean parallel folds; the figures are slender and ceremonious. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
The five nymphs are traditionally read as the five senses, conducting the soul from sensory delight toward intellectual and divine beauty in a Neoplatonic ascent. Their gardens and fountains supply the erotic-contemplative imagery that made the *Hypnerotomachia* a quarry for later allegorists of love and the senses.

## For artists & game designers
The 1499 *Hypnerotomachia* woodcut style, pure outline, minimal hatching, antique architecture and ceremonious figures on open white ground, is an ideal reference for a dream-logic, hermetic-Renaissance visual register: temples, triumphs, hieroglyphic friezes and processions rendered as clean, almost diagrammatic line.

citations.text: Joscelyn Godwin (trans.), *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili: The Strife of Love in a Dream* (Thames & Hudson, 1999).

entries.title: *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Aldus, 1499) — woodcut (leaf 161)
entries.creator: Francesco Colonna (attrib.); printed by Aldus Manutius
entries.rights: Public domain. 1499 Aldine edition via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls within the palace and gardens of the dream: the court of Queen Eleuterylida, the allegorical fountains, the marvellous gardens of glass and silk.

## Iconography
The woodcuts of this region show the architecture and ceremony of the dream-court: colonnaded palaces, ornamental gardens (including the famed gardens of glass and of silk), allegorical fountains and statuary, and processions of attendant figures. Ornament is dense and antiquarian, with vases, candelabra, grotesques and inscribed bases. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
The court of Queen Eleuterylida ('free will') and her marvels stage a moral-philosophical pageant in which the dreamer is offered choices and instructed in the meaning of the figures he meets. The passages model the Renaissance ideal of the garden as a designed allegory, and the cuts furnished motifs to generations of garden, festival and emblem designers.

entries.title: *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Aldus, 1499) — woodcut (leaf 233)
entries.creator: Francesco Colonna (attrib.); printed by Aldus Manutius
entries.rights: Public domain. 1499 Aldine edition via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls among the Triumphs of Love and the temple rites: the processional triumphal cars, the temple of Venus, the sacrifices and nuptial ceremonies.

## Iconography
In this central sequence appear the *Hypnerotomachia*'s great triumphal woodcuts: processional cars (*trionfi*) drawn in classical pomp, the temple of Venus with its altar and priestess, and the rites of sacrifice and betrothal that unite Poliphilo and Polia. Crowds, garlands, musical instruments and antique trophies fill the friezes. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
Modelled on Petrarch's *Trionfi* and on Roman triumphal imagery, these cars and ceremonies are the iconographic heart of the book and among the most copied of all Renaissance processional designs. The temple rites fuse pagan sacrifice with a Neoplatonic sacrament of love, the lovers consecrated before [[motif:venus|Venus]].

## For artists & game designers

entries.title: *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili* (Aldus, 1499) — woodcut (leaf 301)
entries.creator: Francesco Colonna (attrib.); printed by Aldus Manutius
entries.rights: Public domain. 1499 Aldine edition via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls upon the voyage to Cythera: Cupid's barge, the island of Venus, the fountain of Venus and the sacred precinct of the goddess.

## Iconography
Here the woodcuts carry the lovers by Cupid's barge across the sea to the circular island of Cythera, with its concentric gardens, the fountain of Venus at the centre, and the precinct where the goddess is revealed. The island's ideal geometry, a perfect circle of radiating walks, is laid out with diagrammatic clarity. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
The voyage to [[motif:cythera|Cythera]] is the dream's consummation: the island's mandala-like plan figures cosmic harmony and the soul's arrival at the source of beauty, Venus herself unveiled at the centre. This circular paradise garden became a touchstone image of the *locus amoenus*, echoed from Renaissance villa design to Watteau's *Embarkation for Cythera*.

entries.summary:
A woodcut from the *Hypnerotomachia Poliphili*, printed by Aldus Manutius at Venice in 1499 and traditionally attributed to Francesco Colonna, the supreme illustrated incunabulum of the Italian Renaissance. This leaf falls in Book II, where the nymph Polia narrates the lovers' history and the dream draws to its close.

## Iconography
The closing woodcuts accompany Book II, in which the nymph Polia tells the lovers' story from her side: her vow of chastity to Diana, the plague and its terrors, her conversion to Venus, and the dream's dissolution at dawn. The cuts are sparser here, marking key turns of the recollected tale. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
Book II reframes the whole dream as a love-history told by Polia, supplying the human and devotional ground beneath the allegory, chastity surrendered to love, death and plague answered by Venus's mercy. The waking that ends the book returns the reader from vision to loss, the classic melancholy close of the Renaissance dream-poem.

The closing woodcuts accompany Book II, in which the nymph Polia tells the lovers' story from her side: her vow of chastity to Diana, the plague and its terrors, her conversion to Venus, and the dream's dissolution at dawn. The cuts are sparser here, marking key turns of the recollected tale. (The catalogued plate is identified by its sequence within the 1499 edition rather than by a separately verified scene, so this entry characterises the woodcut type at this point in the narrative.)

## Significance
Book II reframes the whole dream as a love-history told by Polia, supplying the human and devotional ground beneath the allegory, chastity surrendered to love, death and plague answered by Venus's mercy. The waking that ends the book returns the reader from vision to loss, the classic melancholy close of the Renaissance dream-poem.

## For artists & game designers
The 1499 *Hypnerotomachia* woodcut style, pure outline, minimal hatching, antique architecture and ceremonious figures on open white ground, is an ideal reference for a dream-logic, hermetic-Renaissance visual register: temples, triumphs, hieroglyphic friezes and processions rendered as clean, almost diagrammatic line.
